<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A group of lawmakers has proposed =
legislation that would allow any investor capable of passing an exam to buy=
 private securities=E2=80=94an array of investments like shares in pre-IPO =
startups or loans to private companies that are considered riskier because =
they have looser disclosure rules than public securities and can be harder,=
 and sometimes impossible, to sell in a pinch.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">The idea is that the ability to make these high-risk, high-reward=
 bets should be open to all sophisticated investors, not just those with th=
e biggest bank accounts. It is a pitch that has lots of fans on Wall Street=
 and many smaller advisory firms, where a movement is trying to get a bigge=
r slice of everyday investors=E2=80=99 retirement funds and savings in priv=
ate-equity funds and other nontraditional investments.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CInvesting your hard-earned money in startup busi=
nesses in your community shouldn=E2=80=99t be limited to the wealthy,=E2=80=
=9D said Sen. Tim Scott (R., S.C.), the ranking member of the Senate Bankin=
g Committee and who proposed the bill.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: =
16px 0;">Crudely speaking the rules in the US are:</p><ol style=3D"list-sty=
le-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"> <li style=3D"margin=
-bottom: 5px;">Ordinary retail investors are only allowed to invest in =E2=
=80=9Cpublic=E2=80=9D investments =E2=80=94=C2=A0stocks, mutual funds, exch=
ange-traded funds, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0that are subject to strict disclosur=
e requirements, so that the investors know what they=E2=80=99re getting int=
o.</li> <li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=E2=80=9CAccredited=E2=80=9D inve=
stors =E2=80=94=C2=A0ones who are sophisticated and able to bear risk =E2=
=80=94 are allowed to invest in whatever they want, without any mandatory d=
isclosure; the accredited investors are assumed to be able to negotiate dis=
closure and governance terms for themselves, to make their own decisions ab=
out how much information they need, and to bear the risk of losing money.</=
